is that context let us discuss that context now so that we understand why as we mentioned in our previous episode is one of the most knowledgeable scholars of islam one of the most knowledgeable scholars from amongst the companions of the prophet sallallahu alaihi wasallam he has many merits and in muslim ahmed the prophet said he said the most knowledgeable of this nation with regards to the laws of prohibit prohibition and lawfulness meaning in the religion of allah is was a very young man in the time of the prophet sallallahu alaihi wasallam despite his youth he was dispatched by the prophet sallallahu alaihi wasallam to entire lands of not muslims to call the people of those lands to islam and to act as a judge and governor of those lands and so was sent to yemen he was sent to yemen in the narration of ibn abbas and also collected by a muslim the famous narration in which the prophet sallallahu alaihi wasallam you arrive in yemen you will come upon a people of the people of the book and the first thing that you are to invite them to and to call them to is to testify that none is rightfully worshipped but allah and then muhammad is the messenger of allah he said and if they agree to this and they submit themselves in this sense then you are to inform them about the five obligatory prayers that allah has made binding upon his

servants etcetera etcetera a well-known narration well adam and jebel died at around the age of 34 early 30s and he died in the plague of amwas which took place in hashem during the time of a few years after the prophet salallahu salem's death and so during the prophet salallahu alaihi wasallam's time in jebel was in his 20s and despite this youth he was already being dispatched and sent to lands as a scholar and representative of the prophet sallallahu alaihi wasallam now that's a little bit of a back story about moab now we understand his stature and his standing amongst the rest of the companions of the prophet sallallahu alaihi wasallam now we discuss this story here that evening is speaking about there's a narration in which this narration many people memorize the wording of this narration but very few people know the backdrop and context of this narration the narration goes as follows the prophet sallallahu alaihi wasallam rebuked and he said to him he asked him rhetorically moab are you somebody who hinders away from the path of allah do you wish to turn people away from allah subhanahu wa ta'ala what is the context of this narration so the prophet sallallahu alaihi wasallam would pray in his mosque would join prayer behind the prophet muhammad sallallahu alaihi wasallam after salat was concluded in the prophet muhammad sallam's mosque and jebel would then go to his village in in medina in the outskirts of

medina and he would find his people there in their local masjid waiting for him and he would come and then he would lead them in salah why did he do this well he did this because he wanted to have the virtue of leading his people in salah but he also wanted to have the virtue of praying behind the prophet muhammad sallallahu alaihi wasallam and in the masjid of the prophet sallallahu alaihi wasallam due to the multiple rewards that a person will get and so we take a number of evidences from this narration but one of them is that the imam can have a different intention in his prayer than the one who is praying behind him right how is that praying obligatory prayer of reisha with the prophet muhammad then he is going to his masjid and he's leading the people in in salah but he's already prayed asia and so he has already prayed isha and so he is counting this prayer as a sunnah as a nafilah as something which is not binding but the people behind him are counting this prayer as the obligatory salah so we see that the imam and the person praying behind the imam they have different intentions but that's okay as long as the imam has the intention to lead others in prayer and so he went a little bit overboard with his zeal and his love for the quran in the sense that he would go

back to his village people are already waiting it's already night time people are tired they're waiting to go home to sleep and they also have fajita which is coming very soon and so muad would arrive there at that masjid only to then oftentimes lead his people in an extremely lengthy prayer some of the time he was narrated to have recited the entirety of surah till baqarah the longest chapter in the quran well there was a young man praying behind and this young man had work and he actually when he saw that the recitation was continuing and continuing and that was reciting and reciting and reciting this man broke out of salah went to a vacant corner of the masjid prayed salah on his own and then he left who finished his prayer others that were present said to muath you know there was this young man he did x y and z that this individual is clearly a hypocrite and that was based on the way that he is seeing it that who would not want to sit there and listen to the quran there there was no way to listen to the quran at that time unless you're in prayer there was no recordings there was no recording of the quran that you could put into a tape recorder or you know listen to on the internet or stream it or something there's no radios the only way that you can listen to the

summary: This lecture is Part 12 of a series on Zadul-Ma'ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) by Ibn al-Qayyim, focusing on the Prophet Muhammad's recitation practices during daily prayers, particularly salat al-Isha. The speaker provides crucial context to a commonly misunderstood hadith about Mu'adh ibn Jabal, explaining that the Prophet's rebuke was specific to Mu'adh leading excessively long prayers (2-3 hours) that discouraged his community, not to moderately longer recitations. The lecture clarifies that while the Prophet recited chapters like Surah Al-Baqarah during some prayers, the context involved specific circumstances and audiences. The speaker emphasizes the difference between what is permissible (mubah) and what constitutes the Sunnah (the Prophet's actual practice), explaining that incomplete chapter recitations or merely tail-end portions of chapters were not part of the Prophet's established practice, despite being technically allowed.
suggested_title: Understanding the Prophet's Quranic Recitation in Prayer: Context and Sunnah Practice
catchy_title: Why the Mu'adh hadith is deeply misunderstood
